evidence: backend/src/api/middleware/roleGuard.ts:17 defines RoleGuardRequest with its own user/db shape, while backend/src/api/middleware/auth.ts:25 and backend/src/permissions/permission-manager.ts:165 define parallel auth request contracts.
roleGuard.ts:57-58 force-casts performer role fields (`as RoleKey`, `as RoleKey[]`) instead of using a shared typed performer/auth context from existing middleware contracts.

roleGuard.ts:46-50 re-queries the performer from DB even though auth middleware already resolves and attaches performer (`auth.ts:117-126`), creating duplicated seam choreography on every guarded route.
permission-manager middleware has the same repeated 'user/db checks + performer lookup' pattern (`permission-manager.ts:194-206`), indicating boundary logic is spread instead of centralized.

roleGuard.ts:47 constructs `new ObjectId(user.userId)` without `ObjectId.isValid`, so malformed IDs throw and drop into generic 500 handler (`roleGuard.ts:86-88`) instead of deterministic auth rejection.
auth middleware already uses a precise guard (`auth.ts:90`) before `new ObjectId`, showing a safer local pattern is available but not applied here.

`backend/src/api/middleware/metrics.ts` is placed under `middleware/` but also owns Prometheus registry bootstrap, metric definitions, health state mutation (`setHealthStatus`), and a full `/metrics` route handler export (`metricsHandler`), mixing transport middleware with observability module responsibilities.

`backend/src/api/middleware/auth.ts` pulls directly from persistence and permission layers (`../../services/db.js`, `../../permissions/permission-manager.js`, `COLLECTIONS.PERFORMERS`) rather than staying a thin transport boundary.

Holistic structure shows `backend/src/api/middleware/` importing across multiple non-API directories (`backend/src/services/`, `backend/src/permissions/`, `backend/src/utils/`, `backend/src/`), indicating the folder is serving as a mixed concern bucket rather than a focused HTTP middleware package.
`auth.ts` combines token parsing/verification, DB performer resolution, permission computation, and request context hydration in one file-level role, so ownership is not crisply domain-aligned.

`metrics.ts` combines instrumentation composition (register/counters/histograms), middleware runtime behavior, endpoint delivery, and health control API, creating a top-level module with multiple reasons to change.

Within the same middleware package, module roles differ sharply (`error.ts` is a focused adapter, while `auth.ts` and `metrics.ts` are orchestration-heavy), which weakens predictability of package boundaries for new changes.

`backend/src/api/routes/voicebot/sessions.ts` remains a 6001-line mixed-responsibility route file: session listing aggregation (`1545-1653`), summary persistence (`4319-4388`), project-file operations (`4390-4475`), and transcript/categorization mutation workflows with event-log side effects (`4724+`) are implemented together.

`app/src/pages/voice/SessionsListPage.tsx` combines URL state migration/persistence (`389-447`), data fetching coordination (`627-650`), FAB global polling/events (`723-750`), and bulk destructive actions (`830-908`) in one page component.

`app/src/store/voiceBotStore.ts` and `app/src/store/sessionsUIStore.ts` both hold UI state plus orchestration/business workflows: socket lifecycle (`969-1107`), HTTP CRUD and uploads (`1438-1710`), ticket mutation logic (`1749+`), and MCP-based title generation with UI notifications (`537-625`).

Voice worker handlers duplicate near-identical fetch/validate/process/update flows across multiple files (`customPrompt.ts`, `questionsHandler.ts`, `oneCustomPrompt.ts`, `createTasksFromChunks.ts`), with divergent processor state shape (`questionsHandler.ts` writes `questioning_error*` root fields while others use `processors_data.<processor>.error*`).

FinOps table components repeat the same month-pin/header/filter-table structural pattern in parallel implementations: `PlanFactGrid.tsx` (`231-352`), `ExpensesGrid.tsx` (`126-260`), and `BonusesGrid.tsx` (`297-409`).

HTTP transport logic is re-implemented in multiple stores/services (`app/src/store/requestStore.ts`, `app/src/store/permissionsStore.ts`, `app/src/store/voicebotHttp.ts`) with separate base-url/proxy/header behavior.

Runtime-scoping entry points are spread across worker/service/tgbot modules (`processingLoop.ts`, `transcribeHandler.ts`, `voicebotWebmDedup.ts`, `ingressHandlers.ts`) instead of one canonical boundary.

CRM/OperOps surfaces still mix legacy snake_case payload fields and newer camelCase conventions (e.g. `app/src/store/projectsStore.ts`, `app/src/store/kanbanStore.ts`, `backend/src/api/routes/crm/tickets.ts`).
Backend route keeps typo-compatible input (`statuses ?? satuses`) in `backend/src/api/routes/crm/tickets.ts`, signaling unstable contract conventions.

Some store actions swallow failures and continue (`projectsStore` fetch methods log+return, `permissionsStore` mutators log+toast without throw, `voiceBotStore` mutators log-only), while sibling actions in the same stores throw.
This creates mixed caller contracts (sometimes rejected Promise, sometimes silent UI toast, sometimes empty fallback).

`usePermissionsStore.updateUserPermissions` posts to `voicebot/permissions/users/permissions`, but backend defines this endpoint as read-only permission retrieval (`backend/src/api/routes/voicebot/permissions.ts`).

`usePermissionsStore.getUserPermissions` calls `permissions/users/permissions` (missing `voicebot/` prefix unlike sibling calls), and store also uses route names not present server-side (`users/access_level`, projects write via `/users/projects` instead of `/users/projects/set`).

Core auth middleware is fail-fast on missing `APP_ENCRYPTION_KEY` (`backend/src/api/middleware/auth.ts`), but miniapp login/auth silently falls back to empty secret (`backend/src/miniapp/routes/index.ts`).
Miniapp `IS_MINIAPP_DEBUG_MODE` path bypasses token verification and injects `testData` as user identity in request pipeline (`backend/src/miniapp/routes/index.ts`).
Repetitive boilerplate request wrappers and near-duplicate proxy/header code appear in multiple files instead of a single shared client.
Many catch blocks are formulaic log/toast blocks with minimal context enrichment in UI stores.
ai_generated_debt.issues_preventing_higher_score: Request and error boilerplate is still copy-pasted across stores instead of centralized abstractions with consistent behavior.

evidence: Runtime-tag migration remains half-removed: runtime scoping helpers currently no-op (`backend/src/services/runtimeScope.ts`), while many runtime paths still call `mergeWithRuntimeFilter` and scripts still backfill `runtime_tag` (`backend/scripts/runtime-tag-backfill.ts`).

Voice upload/session APIs still expose legacy compatibility paths and naming (`/close_session`, `/uploads/*` aliases in `backend/src/api/routes/voicebot/uploads.ts`) while new canonical flows are also present.
Frontend voice log still carries legacy transcript schema fallback (`transcription_chunks`) in `app/src/components/voice/SessionLog.tsx`.

findings.summary: Role guard defines a parallel request/auth contract instead of reusing the canonical authenticated request type.
evidence: roleGuard.ts:17-26 introduces `RoleGuardRequest` with optional `user`/`db` fields that overlap with `AuthenticatedRequest` in auth.ts:25-43 and permission-manager.ts:165-178.
roleGuard.ts:57-58 uses casts (`as RoleKey`, `as RoleKey[]`) because the local type is weaker than the already-established performer/auth types.
findings.suggestion: Unify middleware contracts around a single exported authenticated request/performer type and remove per-middleware request redefinitions; eliminate force-casts by validating role fields at the auth boundary.

findings.summary: Role guard repeats performer lookup that upstream auth middleware already performs, creating noisy and expensive integration seams.
evidence: roleGuard.ts:46-50 performs a DB read for performer each call, despite auth.ts:117-126 already attaching `performer` and `db` onto request.
permission-manager.ts:202-207 repeats the same lookup sequence, reinforcing seam duplication across authorization middlewares.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a shared authorization context resolver (or trust the `req.performer` populated by auth middleware) so role/permission guards consume one resolved identity object instead of reloading it.

findings.summary: Role guard turns malformed user IDs into internal errors instead of explicit authentication failures.
evidence: roleGuard.ts:47 directly calls `new ObjectId(user.userId)`; invalid IDs throw and are caught only by the broad catch block returning 500 (roleGuard.ts:86-88).
auth.ts:90 demonstrates the intended local defensive pattern with `ObjectId.isValid(payload.userId)` before constructing ObjectId.
findings.suggestion: Validate `user.userId` with `ObjectId.isValid` and return 401/400 before DB query; reserve catch-path 500 for true unexpected runtime failures.

findings.summary: The `api/middleware` package is carrying unrelated ownership domains (auth policy+data access and observability runtime), obscuring directory boundaries.
evidence: `metrics.ts` exports `register`, multiple metric primitives, `setHealthStatus`, `metricsMiddleware`, and `metricsHandler`, indicating a full observability module living inside middleware.
`auth.ts` imports DB and permission services and queries performer collection directly, making middleware depend on lower-layer implementation details.
`error.ts` remains a thin transport adapter, highlighting that sibling files in the same folder do not share a coherent package purpose.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: 1) Create `backend/src/observability/httpMetrics.ts` and move Prometheus registry/metric definitions plus `setHealthStatus` there; keep temporary re-exports in `api/middleware/metrics.ts`. 2) Create `backend/src/api/routes/system/metrics.ts` (or existing system routes area) and move `metricsHandler` there; keep middleware-only logic in `api/middleware/metrics.ts`. 3) Extract auth data/policy work from `auth.ts` into `backend/src/services/auth/sessionResolver.ts` (token->performer->permissions), leaving middleware as adapter. 4) Update imports with `rg "api/middleware/(metrics|auth)" backend/src -n` and adjust call sites. 5) Validate with `cd backend && npm run build && npm run test`.

findings.summary: Transport middleware modules are acting as domain orchestrators, reducing ownership clarity and increasing ripple effects for unrelated changes.
evidence: `auth.ts` handles cryptographic verification, persistence lookup, permission derivation, and request model shaping in one module, so auth policy/storage changes force middleware edits.
`metrics.ts` centralizes both HTTP lifecycle interception and global metric/health control concerns, so metric model changes and route exposure changes collide in one file.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: 1) Introduce clear top-level ownership split: `api/middleware/*` for pure request/response adapters, `services/auth/*` for auth resolution, `observability/*` for metric state/model, and `api/routes/system/*` for endpoint exposure. 2) Move logic in this order: auth resolver extraction first, observability extraction second, route handler move third, then delete compatibility re-exports. 3) Run import and boundary checks: `rg "from '../../(services|permissions|utils|constants)" backend/src/api/middleware -n` should shrink to adapter-only dependencies. 4) Validate with `cd backend && npm run build` and smoke route checks for `/api/metrics` plus authenticated endpoints.

findings.summary: Voice sessions route file is a god-module that mixes unrelated HTTP domains and heavy domain workflows in one surface.
evidence: `sessions.ts` is 6001 LOC and declares dozens of route handlers in one file (`router.post` entries from `1659` through `5937`).

The same module handles list/read CRUD (`1545+`), summary save (`4319+`), project file catalog/upload (`4390+`), and transcript/categorization edit/delete flows with event-log orchestration (`4724+`, `5092+`, `5263+`).
Message-specific logic is partially extracted to `messageHelpers.ts`, but high-churn mutation orchestration still lives inline in `sessions.ts`, keeping boundary split incomplete.
findings.suggestion: Split `sessions.ts` by bounded responsibility (e.g., `sessions.list.ts`, `sessions.mutations.ts`, `sessions.project-files.ts`, `sessions.logs.ts`) and move transcript/categorization mutation orchestration into service modules so route handlers become thin transport adapters.

findings.summary: Sessions list page is overloaded with persistence, realtime polling glue, filtering, and mutation workflows beyond page composition responsibility.
evidence: The page holds many unrelated state domains simultaneously (`335-351` local UI action state, query parsing/filtering `371-387`, saved-tags and localStorage migration `389-447`).
It runs global FAB synchronization via interval polling + custom event listeners (`723-750`) while also handling bulk merge/delete orchestration (`830-908`).
It delegates title generation to a store action that itself is workflow-heavy (`sessionsUIStore.ts:537-625`), indicating missing dedicated hook/controller boundaries.
findings.suggestion: Extract focused hooks/controllers: `useSessionsListFilters`, `useSessionsBulkActions`, and `useVoiceFabSync`; keep `SessionsListPage` as composition/render only. Move interval/event sync out of page and into a single reusable voice session activity source.

findings.summary: Voice stores combine transport, domain mutation, UI feedback, and cross-store coupling, creating mixed responsibilities and high-change blast radius.
evidence: `voiceBotStore.ts` owns socket setup/rehydration (`969-1107`), REST fetching (`1438+`), upload transport (`1574+`), optimistic in-store domain edits (`1776+`, `1849+`), and UI notifications (`message.*` calls throughout).
`sessionsUIStore.ts` (nominally UI state) contains MCP network workflow and session title generation logic with backend URL resolution (`537-625`).
Cross-store imperative coupling (`useSessionsUIStore.getState()` inside `voiceBotStore.ts:1091-1093`, `1751`, `1814`) tightens runtime dependencies between state containers.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a service layer (`voiceSessionService`, `voiceTaskflowService`, `voiceSocketController`) and keep stores limited to state transitions. Centralize user notifications in a thin UI feedback adapter so stores stop owning transport+UX policy simultaneously.

findings.summary: Worker handlers duplicate the same orchestration skeleton but persist processor state in inconsistent shapes, signaling missing shared processing abstraction.
evidence: `customPrompt.ts` and `questionsHandler.ts` repeat the same steps: validate IDs, load message/session, check categorization, call OpenAI, write success/error flags (`customPrompt.ts:41-203`, `questionsHandler.ts:52-186`).
`oneCustomPrompt.ts` and `createTasksFromChunks.ts` repeat similar queue/processor state transitions (`oneCustomPrompt.ts:89-150`, `createTasksFromChunks.ts:177-299`).

State schema diverges: `questionsHandler.ts` writes `questioning_error*` top-level fields (`103-106`, `142-145`, `167-170`) while sibling handlers use nested `processors_data.<processor>.error*` fields (`customPrompt.ts:101-104`, `155-158`, `182-185`).
findings.suggestion: Build a shared `runProcessorJob` abstraction that standardizes load/validate/skip/execute/persist/error transitions and enforces one canonical processor-state envelope (`processors_data.<processor>`), then migrate prompt/question/task handlers onto it.

findings.summary: FinOps grids reimplement the same month-pin column architecture in parallel, creating structural duplication and inconsistent evolution risk.
evidence: All three components independently build `pinnedSet + orderedMonths + month header with focus/pin buttons` patterns (`PlanFactGrid.tsx:231-352`, `ExpensesGrid.tsx:126-260`, `BonusesGrid.tsx:297-409`).
Each grid separately implements near-identical left-fixed base columns and month-focused class logic (`PlanFactGrid.tsx:236-324`, `ExpensesGrid.tsx:132-234`, `BonusesGrid.tsx:303-383`).
PlanFact page composes all three grids (`PlanFactPage.tsx:369-409`), so cross-grid UI changes require coordinated manual edits in multiple implementations.
findings.suggestion: Extract a shared FinOps table framework (`buildPinnedMonthColumns`, `MonthHeaderCell`, shared pin/focus state hook, shared filter dropdown primitives) and let each grid provide only domain-specific cell renderers/aggregation logic.

findings.summary: Permissions UI writes against endpoints whose server contracts are read-only or mismatched, producing unreliable admin updates.
evidence: `updateUserPermissions` posts to `voicebot/permissions/users/permissions` (`permissionsStore.ts:165`) but server route `/users/permissions` returns computed permissions and does not persist updates (`permissions.ts:263-291`).
`getUserPermissions` uses `permissions/users/permissions` without `voicebot/` prefix (`permissionsStore.ts:200`) unlike sibling calls in the same store (`permissionsStore.ts:93,111,124,142`).
`updateUserAccessLevel` calls `voicebot/permissions/users/access_level` (`permissionsStore.ts:213`) but no matching backend route exists in `permissions.ts`.
findings.suggestion: Define a single typed contract for permissions endpoints (read vs mutate), align client paths to it, and add compile-time route constants plus integration tests that verify each store action hits an existing backend route with expected response shape.

findings.summary: Miniapp auth path accepts empty JWT secret fallback while core API auth requires explicit secret, creating inconsistent trust boundaries.
evidence: Core middleware throws if `APP_ENCRYPTION_KEY` is missing via `getEncryptionKey()` (`auth.ts:45-50`) and rejects auth.
Miniapp login signs token with `process.env.APP_ENCRYPTION_KEY ?? ''` (`index.ts:135-137`) and auth verifies with same fallback (`index.ts:168-170`), allowing operation with empty secret.
Miniapp debug path bypasses token verification and injects `testData` identity (`index.ts:152-159`).
findings.suggestion: Use the same fail-fast secret resolution utility in miniapp and core auth, disallow empty secrets in all environments, and gate debug bypass behind explicit non-production + additional hard guard (e.g. allowlist or signed debug header).

findings.summary: State stores mix throw-on-error and log-and-continue behavior, so callers cannot reliably handle failures.
evidence: `projectsStore` fetch methods catch and only log/set loading (`projectsStore.ts:74-77`, `95-98`, `116-119`) while CRUD methods rethrow (`187-189`, `203-205`, etc.).
`permissionsStore` mutators mostly swallow errors after toast (`permissionsStore.ts:154-159`, `171-176`, `190-195`, `223-227`, `239-244`), while `loadPermissionsLog` rethrows (`135`).
`voiceBotStore` has mutators with log-only catches (`voiceBotStore.ts:1356-1358`, `1410-1412`) but other flows surface explicit UI error messaging.
findings.suggestion: Adopt one store-level error policy (either always throw and let UI decide, or always return typed Result) and enforce it with shared helper wrappers plus lint rule/tests for swallowed catches.

findings.summary: Multiple independent HTTP wrappers duplicate transport logic and diverge on headers, prefixes, and failure semantics.
evidence: `requestStore` defines its own backend/proxy resolver and POST wrapper (`requestStore.ts:22-45`, `49-92`).
`permissionsStore` re-implements similar backend/proxy logic (`permissionsStore.ts:35-79`) instead of reusing shared client.
`voicebotHttp` adds a third implementation with separate logging/retry checks (`voicebotHttp.ts:42-84`).
A canonical axios client already exists (`api.ts:6-14`) but is bypassed by these wrappers.
findings.suggestion: Consolidate transport into one typed HTTP layer with domain-specific adapters (CRM, voicebot, permissions) and remove per-store URL/proxy logic to prevent drift.

findings.summary: Runtime-tag routing was neutralized but call sites, scripts, and model fields still carry the old mechanism, leaving migration residue in active paths.
evidence: Runtime filter helpers are now effectively no-op (`runtimeScope.ts:129-154`, `156-167`), but multiple runtime paths still call `mergeWithRuntimeFilter(..., { field: 'runtime_tag' })` (`processingLoop.ts:87-92`, `transcribeHandler.ts:137-142`, `voicebotWebmDedup.ts:43-55`, `ingressHandlers.ts:193-194`).
A dedicated backfill script still writes `runtime_tag: 'prod'` (`runtime-tag-backfill.ts:29-34`, `51-54`).
Active mapping type still carries `runtime_tag` as first-class field (`activeSessionMapping.ts:9`).
findings.suggestion: Complete the migration by removing runtime_tag from active domain types/scripts and deleting runtime-filter call sites after introducing explicit environment/DB isolation at composition boundaries.

findings.summary: Public request contracts mix legacy naming and typo compatibility, which keeps conventions unstable across modules.
evidence: Tickets route accepts both `statuses` and misspelled `satuses` (`tickets.ts:208`).
Frontend CRM stores keep snake_case API fields (`project_groups`, `project_group`, `source_project_group`, `dest_project_group`) while other app modules use camelCase typed client style.
The same domain (projects/tickets) therefore presents two naming dialects depending on call path.
findings.suggestion: Freeze a canonical request vocabulary (camelCase or strict backend snake_case with DTO mappers), remove typo aliases, and add validation that rejects unknown keys to prevent further drift.

findings.summary: Auth/role middleware bypass shared API envelope, producing incompatible error shapes
evidence: `auth.ts` and `roleGuard.ts` return `{ error: string }` directly from middleware.
`error.ts` centralizes errors through `sendError`, which returns `{ data: null, error: { message, ... } }`.
findings.suggestion: Route all middleware-originated errors through `sendError` (or a shared auth error helper wrapping it) so all API failures follow one envelope and message/code schema.

findings.summary: Performer lookup DB errors are downgraded into 401 user-not-found responses
evidence: `authMiddleware` catches DB query exceptions in performer fetch and only logs (`Error fetching performer`) before continuing.
Subsequent `!performer` branch returns 401 `User not found in system`, conflating infra failure with auth failure.
findings.suggestion: On DB access failure, return a 500 via `sendError` with stable code (for example `auth_backend_unavailable`) and reserve 401 strictly for verified credential/identity failures.

findings.summary: Role guard lacks ObjectId validity guard and can emit 500 for malformed user IDs
evidence: `roleGuard.ts` calls `new ObjectId(user.userId)` directly.
`auth.ts` checks `ObjectId.isValid(payload.userId)` before conversion; guard is not mirrored in role guard.
findings.suggestion: Validate `user.userId` with `ObjectId.isValid` in `requireRole`; return 401/403 for invalid identity inputs instead of falling into catch-all 500.

findings.summary: Auth request context is duplicated across middleware with drifting optional fields
evidence: `AuthenticatedRequest` and `RoleGuardRequest` both define `user` structures manually.
One interface treats user/db as required while the other marks them optional, forcing downstream defensive checks and drift.
findings.suggestion: Extract shared auth context types into a single module (e.g. `middleware/contracts/authContext.ts`) and compose narrower middleware-specific types from that source.

findings.summary: Sibling middleware uses inconsistent export conventions without clear policy
evidence: `auth.ts` and `roleGuard.ts` expose both named and default exports for middleware functions.
`error.ts` uses named exports only and also introduces the only class type in this folder (`AppError`).
findings.suggestion: Adopt one middleware export convention (named-only recommended for tree-shaking and refactor safety), remove default exports, and keep error type declarations in a dedicated shared error-contract file.

findings.summary: Active-connection gauge can drift because decrement only happens on `finish`
evidence: `metrics.ts` increments `activeConnections` per request and decrements only in `res.on('finish')`.
No `close`/once-guarded fallback path exists for aborted or prematurely closed responses.
findings.suggestion: Track completion with a once-guarded finalize handler bound to both `finish` and `close` so gauge decrement is balanced for all termination paths.

findings.summary: Shared middleware concerns are implemented as parallel ad-hoc patterns
evidence: Auth and role middleware duplicate actor typing and partially duplicate identity loading/validation responsibilities.
Error protocol diverges between middleware, with no single enforced contract despite existing response helpers.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a unified middleware contract layer: shared `AuthContext` type, shared auth error helper (backed by `sendError`), and a single rule for whether role checks trust `req.performer` vs re-querying DB.

findings.summary: Role guard ignores authenticated performer handoff and performs extra DB orchestration
evidence: Auth middleware already resolves performer and attaches it to request.
Role guard performs another performer fetch by userId rather than consuming handoff context, adding extra seam complexity and failure modes.
findings.suggestion: Refactor role guard to consume `req.performer` populated by auth middleware (with explicit fallback policy), and centralize refresh logic in one helper only when strict revalidation is needed.
